Title: cs61a-projects/scheme/scheme.py at master - GitHub
URL: https://github.com/MattZhao/cs61a-projects/blob/master/scheme/scheme.py
Content: """A Scheme interpreter and its read-eval-print loop.""". def scheme\_eval(expr, env, \_=None): # Optional third argument is ignored. >>> expr = read\_line("(+ 2 2)"). >>> scheme\_eval(expr, create\_global\_frame()). return scheme\_atomp(expr) or scheme\_stringp(expr) or expr is okay. def scheme\_apply(procedure, args, env):. """Apply Scheme PROCEDURE to argument values ARGS in environment ENV.""". return apply\_primitive(procedure, args, env). new\_env = make\_call\_frame(procedure, args, env). def eval\_all(expressions, env):. """Evaluate a Scheme list of EXPRESSIONS & return the value of the last.""". scheme\_eval(expressions[x], env). return scheme\_eval(expressions[len(expressions)-1], env, True). def apply\_primitive(procedure, args\_scheme\_list, env):. """Apply PrimitiveProcedure PROCEDURE to ARGS\_SCHEME\_LIST in ENV. args = list(args\_scheme\_list) # Convert a Scheme list to a Python list. def make\_call\_frame(procedure, args, env):. """Make a frame that binds the formal paramters of PROCEDURE to ARGS.""". new\_f = procedure.env.make\_child\_frame(procedure.formals, args). new\_f = env.make\_child\_frame(procedure.formals, args). in a Scheme list of formal parameters FORMALS are bound to the Scheme. a Scheme list of BODY expressions, and a parent environment that. env.bindings[target] = scheme\_eval(expressions[1], env). if scheme\_true(scheme\_eval(expressions[0], env)):. return scheme\_eval(expressions[1],env, True).

Title: Scheme Interpreter | CS 61A Spring 2026
URL: https://cs61a.org/proj/scheme
Content: # Scheme Interpreter * <scheme.zip>. > * Submit with Phase 1 and Phase 2 complete by **Thursday 04/23** (worth 0.5 pt). In this project, you will use Python to implement an interpreter for a subset of the Scheme language. The subset of the language used in this project is described in the functional programming section of Composing Programs, as well as this language specification and built-in procedure reference. Examples from Chapters 1 and 2 are included as test cases for this project. Language features from Chapters 3, 4, and 5 are not part of this project, but of course you are welcome to extend your interpreter to implement more of the language. You can download all of the project code as a zip archive. * `scheme_forms.py`: Python functions for evaluating special forms in Scheme (such as `define`, `lambda`, `and`, `cond`, etc.). * `questions.scm`: Scheme procedures for you to implement.

Title: CS61A Proj 4 - Scheme Interpreter - Misaka's blog
URL: https://www.misaka-9982.com/2022/12/17/CS61A-Proj-4
Content: >>> expr = read_line('(+ 2 2)') >>> expr Pair('+', Pair(2, Pair(2, nil))) >>> scheme_eval(expr, create_global_frame())  4  """  # Evaluate atoms if scheme_symbolp(expr): return env.lookup(expr) elif self_evaluating(expr):  return expr  # All non-atomic expressions are lists (combinations) if not scheme_listp(expr): raise SchemeError('malformed list: {0}'.format(repl_str(expr))) first, rest = expr.first, expr.rest if scheme_symbolp(first) and first in scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMS: return scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMSfirst else:  # BEGIN PROBLEM 3 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" procedure = scheme_eval(first, env) args = rest.map(lambda operand: scheme_eval(operand, env)) return scheme_apply(procedure, args, env)  # END PROBLEM 3 def pair_to_list(pair): if pair is nil: return [] else: return [pair.first] + pair_to_list(pair.rest) ``` |. (define x (+ 1 2)) validate_form(expressions, 2, 2) # Checks that expressions is a list of length exactly 2  # BEGIN PROBLEM 4 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" value = scheme_eval(expressions.rest.first, env) env.define(signature, value)  return signature  # END PROBLEM 4 elif isinstance(signature, Pair) and scheme_symbolp(signature.first): # defining a named procedure e.g.

Title: the lambda evaluation in the python implementation of scheme ...
URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/76429607/the-lambda-evaluation-in-the-python-implementation-of-scheme-interpreter-problem
Content: ### your communities. ### more stack exchange communities. Communities for your favorite technologies. Stack Overflow for Teams is now called **Stack Internal**. ##### Collectives™ on Stack Overflow. # the lambda evaluation in the python implementation of scheme interpreter problem from CS61a. I got stuck with the problem 9 of the scheme project, and I couldn't figure out where I got wrong. And this is the first time I do a big project like this, I find it difficult to debug effectively. This is the code in my github. this is the code of this particular file:. """Evaluate Scheme expression EXPR in Frame ENV. first, rest = expr.first, expr.rest. operator = scheme_eval(expr.first, env). # return scheme_apply(operator, expr.rest, env). operands = expr.rest.map(lambda x: scheme_eval(x, env)). """Apply Scheme PROCEDURE to argument values ARGS (a Scheme list) in. Frame ENV, the current environment.""". # vals = args.map(lambda x: scheme_eval(x, env)). and this is error report I got from the terminal. Find the answer to your question by asking.

Title: Study Guide: Interpreters | CS 61A Spring 2026
URL: https://cs61a.org/study-guide/interpreters
Content: To do this, the interpreter needs to understand how the rules of a language work, and to do that, the interpreter needs to be designed (by a human, usually!) to follow those rules. In CS 61A, we'd like to learn how the interpreter *program* works and understand the ideas that go into an interpreter to reveal the magic behind how a computer makes sense of `(+ 1 2)` in Scheme and `1 + 2` in Python. In the Scheme interpreter, we'd like to take the string of characters `(+ 1 2)` and convert it into a `Pair` structure, `Pair('+', Pair(1, Pair(2, nil)))`. 2. In the **Eval** stage, we evaluate the nested data structure to a value. * If we pass a call expression like `Pair('+', Pair(1, Pair(2, nil)))` into the evaluator, we'll follow the call expression evaluation rules to determine the value of the value of the expression.
